meta.title: Rendering Strategies: SSG, SSR, ISR, and PPR
meta.excerpt: Understand when and how to use Static Site Generation, Server-Side Rendering, Incremental Static Regeneration, and Partial Pre-rendering for optimal performance.

seo.metaTitle: Rendering Strategies: SSG, SSR, ISR, PPR — Next.js Performance
seo.metaDescription: Learn the differences between Static Site Generation, Server-Side Rendering, Incremental Static Regeneration, and Partial Pre-rendering to optimize your Next.js application.
seo.keywords: SSG, SSR, ISR, PPR, rendering strategies, next.js, performance optimization, static generation

blocks.content: Choosing the right rendering strategy is crucial for performance. Next.js gives us multiple options, each optimized for different scenarios. This guide helps you understand when to use each strategy.
Rendering Strategy Decision Matrix

rows: Content rarely changes, fast CDN delivery
Same as articles, pre-rendered at build

blocks.content: Static Site Generation (SSG)

SSG generates all pages at build time. The HTML is pre-rendered once and served from a CDN on every request. This gives the absolute best performance and is the default strategy for content that doesn't change often.
Use SSG when: content is static or rarely changes, you can enumerate all possible routes, performance is critical.
Server-Side Rendering (SSR)

SSR fetches data and renders HTML on every request. It's slower than SSG but allows personalization, authentication, and dynamic data. Use SSR when you need user context, authentication, or dynamic query parameters.
Use SSR when: you need authentication/authorization, content is user-specific, you have dynamic query parameters.
Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)

ISR is a hybrid: pages are pre-rendered like SSG, but revalidated periodically. On-demand revalidation supports updating specific pages when content changes. Best for content that updates occasionally (hours, not seconds).
Use ISR when: content updates occasionally, you want fast CDN delivery with occasional updates, you can tolerate stale content briefly.
Partial Pre-rendering (PPR) - Future

PPR (coming to Next.js) allows mixing static and dynamic segments in the same page. Static parts are pre-rendered; dynamic parts render on-demand. This enables fast initial page loads with dynamic personalization.
Future use case: Article page (static) with dynamic comments section (user-specific) in a single route.
Implementation Examples
Here's how we apply these strategies across our application:

items.title: Article Pages (/articles/[slug])
items.description: SSG with generateStaticParams() — pages built once, served from CDN

items.title: Tutorial Pages (/tutorials/[slug])
items.description: SSG with generateStaticParams() — fast, reliable, no server load

items.title: News Hub (/news)
items.description: ISR with 1-hour revalidation — aggregates feeds, updates daily

items.title: Admin Dashboard (/dashboard/admin)
items.description: SSR with auth checks — user-specific, personalized, protected

items.title: Search Results (/search?q=...)
items.description: SSR with dynamic query — personalized search ranking per user

blocks.title: Default to SSG, Optimize from There
blocks.content: Start with SSG for all content. Move to ISR if you need occasional updates. Use SSR only when you truly need per-request personalization. This mindset gives you the best balance of performance and freshness.
